subConcepts.name: Front Placement — Word Goes in Front

learningGoal: How to find a word that goes IN FRONT of another to make a compound word — like snapping two LEGO bricks together!
A handy trick called Say It, Spell It that makes testing combinations super quick

screens.title: What goes IN FRONT of "light"?
screens.body:
Daisy is working on compound word questions.

**Did you know?** A compound word is just two smaller words stuck together — like "sunlight" = sun + light. Your job is to find ONE word that makes **two** real compound words. It's like finding a key that unlocks two doors!

Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'burn' to make two compound words?

steps.why: Find a word that makes a real compound word with "light"
The SAME word must also work with "burn"

screens.title: Say It, Spell It — Front Placement
screens.body: Let's find a word that goes in front of **"light"** and **"burn"**. Here's the trick: try each option **before** the given word and say it in your head. Does it sound like a real word? If it does, you might be on to something!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Say it, check both words, then spell it. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by trying options in front, then check BOTH target words.

steps.why: Say it — does it sound right? Yes!
Both must work — and this one does too!

steps.text: Spell it: SUNLIGHT — one real word ✓
steps.why: It's one word, correctly spelt

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word goes in front of both 'bow' and 'drop' to make two compound words?

Remember: the answer must work with **both** words!

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'bow' and 'drop' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Rain' + 'bow' = rainbow, and 'rain' + 'drop' = raindrop. Both are real compound words. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "rain". 'Rain' + 'bow' = rainbow, and 'rain' + 'drop' = raindrop. Both are real compound words. ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: You've got the front placement recipe!
screens.body: Nice work! Here's your go-to method when the word goes IN FRONT:

steps.text: 1. Put each option BEFORE the given word
steps.why: Say it in your head — sun+light? moon+light?

steps.text: 2. Check it works with BOTH target words
steps.why: The answer must make TWO real compound words
One real word, not two separate words ✓

learningGoal: The sneaky trap where a word works with ONE target but not BOTH
A quick double-check trick that stops you losing easy marks

screens.title: Does "snow" work with both words?
screens.body:
Evie is checking her friend's VR answers.

The question asks: **Which word goes in front of both 'bow' and 'drop'?**

Someone chose **"snow"** — but hang on, does it actually work with BOTH words? Let's play detective!

steps.why: Is "snowbow" a real word?
Is "snowdrop" a real word?

screens.title: Watch out — the BOTH-words trap!
screens.body:
Here's the number one trap in compound word questions: picking a word that only works with ONE of the two targets. Don't fall for it!

'Snowdrop' is real, but 'snowbow' isn't — 'snow' only works with 'drop', not with 'bow'

steps.why: This one might work...
'Snowdrop' is real, but 'snowbow' isn't — 'snow' only works with 'drop', not with 'bow'
rain+bow AND rain+drop

screens.title: Find the word that works with BOTH!
screens.body: Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'burn'?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'burn'?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Sun' + 'light' = sunlight, and 'sun' + 'burn' = sunburn. Both are real compound words. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "sun". 'Sun' + 'light' = sunlight, and 'sun' + 'burn' = sunburn. Both are real compound words. ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: Golden rule: always check BOTH words
screens.body: Now you know the most common trap — and you won't fall for it! Remember:

steps.text: 1. Try each option with the FIRST target word
steps.why: Does it make a real compound?

steps.text: 2. Then check the SECOND target word
steps.why: Does the SAME option work here too?

steps.text: 3. Only pick it if BOTH are real compound words
steps.why: One out of two isn't enough! ✓

subConcepts.name: Back Placement — Word Goes at the End

learningGoal: How to find a word that slots in AFTER another to complete a compound word
Using Say It, Spell It to test back-placement combos like a pro

screens.title: What goes AFTER "bed" and "class"?
screens.body:
Grace is practising back-placement compound words.

This time, the mystery word goes at the **end** — like the last piece of a jigsaw. Can you find the ONE word that completes **both** compound words?

Which word goes after both 'bed' and 'class' to make two compound words?

bodyParts.content:
Grace is practising back-placement compound words.

This time, the mystery word goes at the **end** — like the last piece of a jigsaw. Can you find the ONE word that completes **both** compound words?

Which word goes after both 'bed' and 'class' to make two compound words?

screens.title: Say It, Spell It — Back Placement
screens.body: Let's find a word that goes after **"bed"** and **"class"** to make compound words. Try each option **after** the given words — say it in your head and see if it clicks!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect! Try it after both words, then spell to check. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — start by trying options after the first word, then check the second.

steps.why: Say it — sounds like a real word!
Works with the second word too!
Both are one real word each

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word goes after both 'tooth' and 'hair' to make two compound words?

Remember: put each option **after** both given words.

bodyParts.content:
Which word goes after both 'tooth' and 'hair' to make two compound words?

Remember: put each option **after** both given words.

interaction.question: Which word goes after both 'tooth' and 'hair' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Tooth' + 'brush' = toothbrush, and 'hair' + 'brush' = hairbrush. Both are real compound words. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "brush". 'Tooth' + 'brush' = toothbrush, and 'hair' + 'brush' = hairbrush. Both are real compound words. ✓

screens.title: You've nailed back placement!
screens.body: Brilliant — here's your recipe for when the word goes at the END:

steps.text: 1. Put each option AFTER the given words
steps.why: bed+room? bed+time? bed+side?

steps.text: 2. Check it works with BOTH given words
steps.why: The answer must complete TWO compound words

steps.text: 3. Spell them out to make sure
steps.why: Real compound words, correctly spelt ✓

learningGoal: Why back-placement traps are so sneaky — and how to dodge them
A simple check that stops you picking a word that only fits one pairing

screens.title: Is "paste" really the answer?
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting a mistake in her practice answers.

The question: **Which word goes after both 'tooth' and 'hair'?**

Someone picked **"paste"** — sounds right, doesn't it? But does it actually work with BOTH words? Let's find out!

bodyParts.content:
Daisy is spotting a mistake in her practice answers.

The question: **Which word goes after both 'tooth' and 'hair'?**

Someone picked **"paste"** — sounds right, doesn't it? But does it actually work with BOTH words? Let's find out!

screens.title: One out of two won't do!
screens.body:
'Hairpaste' isn't a real word — 'paste' only works with 'tooth'

You need a word that works with **both** given words — one out of two isn't good enough!

steps.why: 'Hairpaste' isn't a real word — 'paste' only works with 'tooth'
"tooth" + "brush" = real word!
Both compound words are real

screens.title: Find the one that works with BOTH!
screens.body: Which word goes after both 'foot' and 'basket' to make two compound words?

bodyParts.content: Which word goes after both 'foot' and 'basket' to make two compound words?

interaction.question: Which word goes after both 'foot' and 'basket' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Foot' + 'ball' = football, and 'basket' + 'ball' = basketball. Both are real compound words. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "ball". 'Foot' + 'ball' = football, and 'basket' + 'ball' = basketball. Both are real compound words. ✓

screens.title: You're a trap-spotter now!
screens.body: You've cracked the back-placement trap — here's what to remember:

steps.text: toothpaste is real BUT hairpaste is NOT
steps.why: Paste only works with tooth

steps.text: toothbrush is real AND hairbrush is real
steps.why: Brush works with both!

steps.text: Always test BOTH pairings before you choose
steps.why: Two real compound words needed ✓

learningGoal: Why nature words like rain, snow, sun and water are absolute compound word superstars
How to build a secret weapon — a mental bank of nature compounds you can recall in seconds

screens.title: How many compounds does "snow" make?
screens.body:
Aisha is learning nature compound words for her VR test.

**Did you know?** Nature words like **sun**, **rain**, **snow** and **water** are compound word superstars! They combine with LOADS of other words — more than almost anything else in English.

**"snow"** makes: snowball, snowflake, snowman, snowdrop... and more!

screens.title: Nature words = your secret weapon
screens.body: Let's try **"snow"** with **"ball"** and **"man"**. Whenever you see a compound word question, always ask yourself: **could a nature word be the answer?** It's like having a cheat code — sun, rain, snow and water work SO often!

steps.why: Say it — sounds right!
Works with both — that's the one!
Nature words are incredibly versatile ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word goes in front of both 'fall' and 'proof' to make two compound words?

Think: could a nature word work here?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'fall' and 'proof' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Water' + 'fall' = waterfall, and 'water' + 'proof' = waterproof. Water is a great compound-maker! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "water". 'Water' + 'fall' = waterfall, and 'water' + 'proof' = waterproof. Water is a great compound-maker! ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: Your nature compound cheat sheet
screens.body: Brilliant work! Keep these nature compound-makers stored in your memory bank:

steps.text: SUN: sunlight, sunrise, sunset, sunburn, sunflower
steps.why: Sun goes in front
Rain goes in front
Water is incredibly versatile ✓

learningGoal: Why nature words are brilliant — but they don't work EVERY time
How to avoid the trap of forcing a nature word where it doesn't belong

screens.title: Is "rain" always the answer?
screens.body:
Daisy is spotting nature word traps.

Nature words like sun, rain and water make loads of compounds — but here's the catch: they don't work **every** time!

Does **"rain"** really work with both **"storm"** and **"bolt"**? Let's see...

bodyParts.content:
Daisy is spotting nature word traps.

Nature words like sun, rain and water make loads of compounds — but here's the catch: they don't work **every** time!

Does **"rain"** really work with both **"storm"** and **"bolt"**? Let's see...

screens.title: Even superstars have limits!
screens.body:
Just because a word is a common compound-maker doesn't mean it works everywhere — even superstars have off days!

'Rainbolt' isn't a real word — 'rain' works with 'storm' but not 'bolt'

steps.why: It might sound right...
'Rainbolt' isn't a real word — 'rain' works with 'storm' but not 'bolt'
thunder+storm AND thunder+bolt

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word goes in front of both 'colour' and 'melon'?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'colour' and 'melon'?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! 'Water' + 'colour' = watercolour, and 'water' + 'melon' = watermelon. Both are real compound words. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "water". 'Water' + 'colour' = watercolour, and 'water' + 'melon' = watermelon. Both are real compound words. ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: Smart, not automatic!
screens.body: Nature words are your best first guess — but you've got this: always double-check!

steps.text: Nature words are a great FIRST guess
steps.why: Sun, rain, water, snow — try them first

steps.text: But they don't work with EVERYTHING
steps.why: snowring? rainbolt? Not real!

steps.text: Always test BOTH pairings before choosing
steps.why: Say it, spell it, check it ✓

learningGoal: Why body part words like head, hand, foot and eye are brilliant compound builders
How to build up a mental word bank so you can spot them in a flash

screens.title: Compound words with "hand"
screens.body:
Grace is learning body part compound words.

**Did you know?** Body part words are another group of compound word superstars! **"hand"** alone makes: handbag, handshake, handwriting, handful.

Next time you see a compound question, check the options — a body part word might be hiding in there!

screens.title: Body words = compound gold
screens.body: Let's try **"hand"** with **"bag"** and **"shake"**. Body words like head, hand, foot and eye pop up all the time in compound word questions. Once you know their families, you'll spot the answer in seconds!

steps.why: Say it — yes, that's a real word!
Works with both target words!
The more you know, the faster you'll spot them ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word goes in front of both 'brow' and 'lash' to make two compound words?

Think: could a body part word be the answer?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'brow' and 'lash' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Eye' + 'brow' = eyebrow, and 'eye' + 'lash' = eyelash. Body words are compound superstars! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "eye". 'Eye' + 'brow' = eyebrow, and 'eye' + 'lash' = eyelash. Body words are compound superstars! ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: Your body word cheat sheet
screens.body: Fantastic! Here are the body word families to keep in your back pocket:

steps.why: Head goes in front
Hand goes in front
Body words are compound superstars ✓

learningGoal: Why home and building words like door, house and room are great compound builders
How to spot house and room compound families quickly in the exam

screens.title: Compound words with "door"
screens.body:
Daisy is learning home compound words.

**Did you know?** Words about **homes and buildings** are another set of compound word champions! **"door"** alone makes: doorbell, doorstep, doorway, doorknob.

Let's learn how to spot these in a flash.

screens.title: Home words love making compounds
screens.body: Let's try **"door"** with **"bell"** and **"step"**. Home-related words like door, house, room and book are really common in compound word questions — think of all the things you see around the house every day!

steps.why: Say it — real word!
Works with both!
The more compounds you know, the faster you are ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Which word goes in front of both 'shelf' and 'worm' to make two compound words?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'shelf' and 'worm' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Book' + 'shelf' = bookshelf, and 'book' + 'worm' = bookworm. Both use 'book' at the front! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "book". 'Book' + 'shelf' = bookshelf, and 'book' + 'worm' = bookworm. Both use 'book' at the front! ✓

steps.why: Must make a real compound word
The SAME word must work here too!

screens.title: Your home compound toolkit
screens.body: Great job! Here are the home and building families to remember:

steps.why: Door goes in front
House goes in front
Room usually goes at the end ✓

learningGoal: The sneaky imposters: words that LOOK like compounds but totally aren't (is 'carpet' really 'car' + 'pet'?!)
A quick meaning test that tells you if a compound is real or fake

screens.title: Is "carpet" really a compound word?
screens.body:
Aisha is learning about fake compound words.

Here's a brain-tickler: some words LOOK like they're made of two smaller words, but they're actually imposters! **"carpet"** looks like it could be **"car"** + **"pet"**.

But is it really? Let's bust this myth!

screens.title: The meaning test — your fake-detector
screens.body: So is **"carpet"** a real compound word? Here's the test: a REAL compound word's meaning comes from BOTH its parts. If the meaning doesn't connect to both halves, it's an imposter!

interaction.sentence: A real compound word gets its meaning from ____ of its parts
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! A real compound word's meaning comes from BOTH parts. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — a real compound word's meaning must come from BOTH of its parts!

steps.why: 'Carpet' is NOT 'car' + 'pet' — it has nothing to do with cars or pets! It comes from a completely different word origin.
'Carpark' IS a real compound: 'car' + 'park' — a place where you park cars

steps.text: Real compounds: meaning comes from BOTH parts
steps.why: A seashell IS a shell from the sea ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which of these is a REAL compound word using 'butter'?

Remember: the meaning must come from BOTH parts!

interaction.question: Which of these is a REAL compound word using 'butter'?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Butter' + 'cup' = buttercup — a yellow wildflower. The meaning comes from both parts! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "buttercup". 'Butter' + 'cup' = buttercup — a yellow wildflower. The meaning comes from both parts! ✓

screens.title: You can spot the fakes now!
screens.body: Well done — you've got the fake-detecting superpower! Here's how it works:

steps.text: Real: seashell = shell from the sea
steps.why: Meaning comes from BOTH parts
Nothing to do with cars or pets!

steps.text: The meaning test: do both halves contribute?
steps.why: If not, it just looks like a compound ✓

learningGoal: How to find ONE word that links with three others — like finding the centre of a web!
A step-by-step testing method so you never get stuck

screens.title: One word, THREE compounds!
screens.body:
Daisy is finding a word that links three others.

This one's a bit trickier — you need to find ONE word that makes a compound with **all three** given words. It's like finding the one puzzle piece that connects everything!

Which word goes with **"light"**, **"rise"**, AND **"flower"**?

bodyParts.content:
Daisy is finding a word that links three others.

This one's a bit trickier — you need to find ONE word that makes a compound with **all three** given words. It's like finding the one puzzle piece that connects everything!

Which word goes with **"light"**, **"rise"**, AND **"flower"**?

screens.title: Test each option against ALL three
screens.body: We need a word that works with **"light"**, **"rise"**, and **"flower"**. Try each option with all three target words — the right answer must make a real compound word with **every single one**. No shortcuts here!

steps.why: First target — real word!
Second target — also works!
All three work — that's the answer! ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which ONE word makes a compound with all three: 'light', 'rise', and 'flower'?

Test each option against ALL three words!

bodyParts.content:
Which ONE word makes a compound with all three: 'light', 'rise', and 'flower'?

Test each option against ALL three words!

interaction.question: Which ONE word makes a compound with all three: 'light', 'rise', and 'flower'?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Sun' + 'light' = sunlight, 'sun' + 'rise' = sunrise, 'sun' + 'flower' = sunflower. Sun links all three! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "sun". 'Sun' + 'light' = sunlight, 'sun' + 'rise' = sunrise, 'sun' + 'flower' = sunflower. Sun links all three! ✓

screens.title: Three-way links — you've got this!
screens.body: Brilliant work! For three-way compound questions, just follow these steps:

steps.text: 1. Try each option with the FIRST target word
steps.why: Does it make a real compound?

steps.text: 2. If yes, try it with the SECOND and THIRD
steps.why: It must work with ALL of them

steps.text: 3. If any pairing fails, move to the next option
steps.why: Keep going until all three work ✓

learningGoal: How to split a compound word back into its two parts — like cracking a code!
A split-and-check method that makes reverse questions feel easy

screens.title: Can you split "playground"?
screens.body:
Grace is practising splitting compound words.

Here's a fun twist — sometimes the question works **backwards**! You're given a compound word and you need to crack it apart into its two pieces.

Where does **"playground"** split?

screens.title: Split and check — crack it open!
screens.body: Let's split the word **"playground"**. Your mission: find the exact point where it divides into **two real words**. Both halves must be genuine English words!

interaction.sentence: A compound word is made of ____ smaller words joined together
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! A compound word is two smaller words joined together. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — a compound word is made of TWO smaller words joined together!

steps.why: Look for two real words hiding inside
Both are real words: "play" and "ground"

steps.text: Meaning: a ground (area) where you play
steps.why: The meaning comes from both parts ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
How does 'playground' split into two words?

Remember: both halves must be real English words!

interaction.question: How does 'playground' split into two words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Playground' splits into 'play' + 'ground' — an area (ground) where children play. Both halves are real words! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "play + ground". 'Playground' splits into 'play' + 'ground' — an area (ground) where children play. Both halves are real words! ✓

screens.title: Compound splitter — level unlocked!
screens.body: You're getting really good at this! To split any compound word:

steps.text: 1. Look for a natural split point
steps.why: Where could two real words be hiding?

steps.text: 2. Check BOTH halves are real words
steps.why: 'play' and 'ground' — yes! 'pla' and 'yground' — no!

steps.text: 3. Check the meaning connects both parts
steps.why: A playground IS a ground for playing ✓

learningGoal: Why direction words like over, under, out, up and down are compound-making machines
How knowing these gives you a massive head start in the exam

screens.title: Direction words are compound superstars!
screens.body:
Aisha is learning direction compound words.

**Did you know?** Words like **over, under, out, up, down** are some of the busiest compound-makers in the whole of English! **"over"** alone makes: overcoat, overnight, overcome, overlook.

Spotting a direction word in the options? That's often your answer!

screens.title: Direction words = quick wins
screens.body: Let's try **"over"** with **"coat"** and **"night"**. Direction words like over, under, out, up, down and back pop up ALL the time in compound questions. Spot one in the options? Try it first!

steps.why: Say it — real word!
Works with both!
Direction words are incredibly versatile ✓

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Which word goes in front of both 'coat' and 'night' to make two compound words?

Check the options — is there a direction word?

bodyParts.content:
Which word goes in front of both 'coat' and 'night' to make two compound words?

Check the options — is there a direction word?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'coat' and 'night' to make two compound words?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Over' + 'coat' = overcoat, and 'over' + 'night' = overnight. Direction words make great compounds! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "over". 'Over' + 'coat' = overcoat, and 'over' + 'night' = overnight. Direction words make great compounds! ✓

screens.title: Your direction word power-ups
screens.body: Excellent! These direction compound families are your ticket to fast answers:

steps.why: Over goes in front
Under goes in front
Direction words are easy wins ✓

learningGoal: A speed strategy for compound questions so you can blitz through them in the exam
Which options to test first — because smart order saves precious seconds

screens.title: Solve it in seconds!
screens.body:
Evie is learning to solve compound words quickly.

In the real exam, every second counts! The secret? Don't test all five options randomly — be smart about which ones you try first.

Which word goes in front of both 'card' and 'man'?

bodyParts.content:
Evie is learning to solve compound words quickly.

In the real exam, every second counts! The secret? Don't test all five options randomly — be smart about which ones you try first.

Which word goes in front of both 'card' and 'man'?

screens.title: The speed strategy — work smart!
screens.body: We need a word for **"card"** and **"man"**. Here's the trick: when time is tight, try the most likely answers first! Common short words (sun, rain, hand, foot, over, under) are right way more often than unusual long words.

steps.why: These are the most common compound-makers

steps.text: Step 2: Test the shortest, most common option first
steps.why: Short everyday words are usually right
Found it on the first try!

screens.title: Your turn — be quick!
screens.body:
Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'shine'?

Scan the options — which looks most likely?

bodyParts.content:
Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'shine'?

Scan the options — which looks most likely?

interaction.question: Which word goes in front of both 'light' and 'shine'?
interaction.correctFeedback: Brilliant! 'Moon' + 'light' = moonlight, and 'moon' + 'shine' = moonshine. Nature words are common compound-makers! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is "moon". 'Moon' + 'light' = moonlight, and 'moon' + 'shine' = moonshine. Nature words are common compound-makers! ✓

screens.title: Speed mode — unlocked!
screens.body: You've got the speed strategy now! To solve compound words FAST in the exam:

steps.why: Nature, body, direction, home words

steps.text: 2. Test the most likely option with BOTH targets
steps.why: Don't check all five — start with the best guess

steps.text: 3. If it works with both, move on!
steps.why: Confidence + speed = more marks ✓

subConcepts.name: GL Compound Pairs — Pick From Two Groups

learningGoal: How to solve the real GL compound word format — picking one word from each of two groups
The 3×3 Grid Method: systematically test all 9 combinations to find the one that makes a real word
Why spelling matters more than sound for tricky compounds

screens.title: Evie's two-group challenge
screens.body:
Evie is tackling GL compound word questions. This time there are **two groups of words** — pick one from each group to make a single compound word.

Group A: ( rain   silk   brass )
Group B: ( bow   thread   ring )

That's **9 possible combinations** to check! How do you find the right one quickly?

screens.title: The 3×3 Grid Method
screens.body: Start with the **first word in Group A** and test it with **each word in Group B**:

steps.why: rathread? Not a word.
rainring? Not a word.
rainbow — YES! That's a real word!
Start with the first word in Group A and test it with each word in Group B

screens.title: The Spelling Secret
screens.body:
For **tricky compounds**, the word you make sounds **completely different** from the two parts.

The GL test loves this trick! The key is to **spell combinations out** letter by letter, not say them aloud.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body:
Find two words, one from each group, that join to make a new word.

Group A: ( cup   jug   pot )
Group B: ( board   handle   lid )

interaction.question: Which combination makes a real compound word?

screens.title: Two-group compound words — sorted!
screens.body: You now know how to solve the GL's compound word format! Remember:

steps.why: Test it against all 3 Group B words

steps.text: 2. Move to Group A, word 2, then 3
steps.why: Systematically check all 9 combos
Hard questions change pronunciation: habit+at = habitat

steps.text: 4. Group A word always goes FIRST
steps.why: Never try Group B word first — the order is fixed
